probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, and Brier score separately for each of the 6 ethnicity groups. Compute disparity metrics (max-min gap, standard deviation across groups, equalized odds difference). Healthy signal: performance gaps < 0.05 AUROC across groups with overlapping bootstrap confidence intervals. Unhealthy: one or more groups (typically minority: Hispanic, Asian, Black) show AUROC > 0.05 lower than majority group, indicating systematic disparity. Apply stratified bootstrapping to quantify uncertainty given small subgroup sizes.
possible_sources: Chen et al. 'Ethical Machine Learning in Healthcare' (Annual Review of Biomedical Data Science, 2021)
Seyyed-Kalantari et al. 'Underdiagnosis bias of AI algorithms' (Nature Medicine, 2021)
MIMIC-III benchmark by Harutyunyan et al. 2019
Fairlearn toolkit

probe_designs.content: Since output is mortality probability, evaluate calibration globally and per ethnicity group using reliability diagrams, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Hosmer-Lemeshow test. Check if predicted probabilities match observed mortality rates within each decile. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 across all subgroups, calibration curves near diagonal. Unhealthy: systematic over/under-prediction for specific ethnic groups, indicating miscalibration that could bias clinical decisions differently across populations.
possible_sources: Guo et al. 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Pfohl et al. 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction' (JBI 2021)
Barda et al. 'Addressing bias in prediction models' (Nature Medicine 2021)

probe_designs.content: Audit the joint distribution of mortality label and ethnicity. Check: (1) class imbalance ratio (ICU mortality is typically 10-15%), (2) sample size per ethnicity group, (3) mortality prevalence within each ethnicity. Healthy: documented imbalance addressed with stratified sampling, class weighting, or focal loss. Unhealthy: severe underrepresentation of minority ethnicities (<100 samples) combined with different base rates, which causes the model to default to majority-group priors and amplifies bias.
possible_sources: Johnson et al. 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database' (Scientific Data 2016)
Harutyunyan et al. 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data' 2019
Rajkomar et al. 'Ensuring Fairness in Machine Learning to Advance Health Equity' (Annals of Internal Medicine 2018)

probe_designs.content: Plot training and validation loss (binary cross-entropy) per epoch. Check for overfitting given the high-dimensional 10,000-dim TF-IDF input with potentially limited ICU samples. Healthy: validation loss decreases monotonically then plateaus; small train-val gap. Unhealthy: validation loss diverges from training loss after a few epochs (overfitting to sparse TF-IDF features), or train loss fails to decrease (underfitting due to poor feature representation). Recommend early stopping based on validation AUROC.
possible_sources: Goodfellow et al. 'Deep Learning' textbook Chapter 7
Prechelt 'Early Stopping - But When?' 1998
Caruana et al. 'Overfitting in Neural Nets' NIPS 2000

probe_designs.content: Test whether the model inappropriately relies on the ethnicity one-hot vector. Run three evaluations: (1) with true ethnicity, (2) with ethnicity set to majority class for all, (3) with ethnicity randomized. Measure AUROC shift and per-patient prediction change. Healthy: small predictive shift, indicating ethnicity is used as a minor covariate, not a primary signal. Unhealthy: large AUROC drop or high flip rate when ethnicity is altered, indicating the model encodes ethnicity-based shortcuts rather than clinical features.
possible_sources: Kusner et al. 'Counterfactual Fairness' (NeurIPS 2017)
Pfohl et al. 'Counterfactual reasoning for fair clinical risk prediction' (MLHC 2019)
Geirhos et al. 'Shortcut Learning in Deep Neural Networks' (Nature Machine Intelligence 2020)

probe_designs.probe_name: TF-IDF Feature Importance and Clinical Plausibility
probe_designs.content: Extract top-k most influential TF-IDF tokens for mortality prediction using coefficient weights (linear) or SHAP/LIME (nonlinear). Check clinical plausibility: expect tokens like 'cardiac arrest', 'sepsis', 'intubation', 'DNR'. Stratify importance by ethnicity subgroup to detect group-specific spurious correlations. Healthy: top features are clinically meaningful and consistent across subgroups. Unhealthy: top features include demographic markers, artifacts (e.g., template phrases, provider names), or subgroup-specific shortcuts such as hospital location terms.
possible_sources: Ribeiro et al. 'Why Should I Trust You?: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier' (KDD 2016)
Lundberg & Lee 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions' (NeurIPS 2017)
Caruana et al. 'Intelligible Models for Healthcare' (KDD 2015)

probe_designs.content: Decompose the aggregate binary cross-entropy loss into per-ethnicity components weighted by subgroup size. Track these during training. Healthy: all subgroup losses decrease at similar rates and converge to similar values. Unhealthy: minority-group loss plateaus early or oscillates while majority-group loss continues decreasing, indicating the optimizer is dominated by majority gradients. Suggests need for group-reweighted loss, DRO (distributionally robust optimization), or balanced batch sampling.
possible_sources: Sagawa et al. 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks for Group Shifts' (ICLR 2020)
Byrd & Lipton 'What is the Effect of Importance Weighting in Deep Learning?' (ICML 2019)
Hashimoto et al. 'Fairness Without Demographics in Repeated Loss Minimization' (ICML 2018)

probe_designs.probe_name: Input Perturbation and Note Length Sensitivity
probe_designs.content: Test robustness by perturbing TF-IDF inputs: (1) drop 10-30% of non-zero features randomly, (2) add Gaussian noise, (3) truncate to shorter note lengths. Evaluate per-subgroup AUROC degradation. Healthy: gradual, similar degradation across groups, indicating reliance on distributed signal. Unhealthy: sharp performance collapse under perturbation, or asymmetric degradation where one ethnicity group degrades faster, indicating fragile or group-specific feature reliance. Also check correlation between note length and prediction confidence across groups.
possible_sources: Hendrycks & Dietterich 'Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness' (ICLR 2019)
Wallace et al. 'Universal Adversarial Triggers for NLP' (EMNLP 2019)
Zhang et al. 'Hurtful Words: Quantifying Biases in Clinical Contextual Word Embeddings' (CHIL 2020)

probe_designs.content: Run k-fold stratified cross-validation (stratified by both mortality and ethnicity) with at least 5 random seeds. Report mean ± std of global and per-subgroup AUROC. Healthy: standard deviation < 0.02 AUROC across folds/seeds, stable ranking of subgroup performance. Unhealthy: high variance (>0.05 AUROC), particularly for minority subgroups, indicating the model is unstable and findings of bias may be non-reproducible. Also test via bootstrap confidence intervals whether observed fairness gaps are statistically significant.
possible_sources: Bouthillier et al. 'Accounting for Variance in Machine Learning Benchmarks' (MLSys 2021)
Cooper et al. 'Variance, Self-Consistency, and Arbitrariness in Fair Classification' (ICML 2024)
scikit-learn StratifiedKFold documentation

probe_designs.content: With 10,000-dim TF-IDF features, check the n/p ratio. If ICU cohort is ~10,000-50,000 patients, the model risks underdetermination. Diagnostic: (1) rank of training feature matrix, (2) condition number, (3) performance vs. number of top-k features retained via mutual information / chi-squared filtering, (4) comparison against L1-regularized logistic regression baseline. Healthy: model performance saturates well before using all 10k features; sparse coefficients. Unhealthy: training AUROC near 1.0 with unstable validation metrics, indicating memorization. Suggests dimensionality reduction (SVD, LSA) or sparsity constraints.
possible_sources: Tibshirani 'Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso' (JRSS 1996)
Ng 'Feature selection, L1 vs L2 regularization' (ICML 2004)
Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman 'The Elements of Statistical Learning' Ch.18 High-Dimensional Problems
